title: Number of ZigZag Arrays II
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: Pareto-optimal challenge: the C++ linear-recurrence solution is the recommended proposal, the speed extreme, and the memory extreme. The direct matrix-exponentiation solution from the hint is correct but dominated here by the recurrence recovered from the same fixed transition operator.

variants.languageReason: The value domain has m <= 75 while n can be 1e9. C++ gives native modular arithmetic, compact stack arrays for the short DP prefix, and low-overhead vector storage for the recovered recurrence and Kitamasa polynomials. This avoids the much larger constant cost of 2m x 2m matrix exponentiation while keeping the code compatible with LeetCode's C++ class signature.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected. It gives the best practical mix of native modular arithmetic, concise Berlekamp-Massey/Kitamasa code, fixed small arrays, and direct LeetCode method support.

Native and slightly lower-level, but LeetCode's C++ signature is more ergonomic and the O(m) explicit memory is already tiny; manual polynomial buffers would add risk without a meaningful footprint win.
Native performance is viable, but mutable polynomial reductions and modular recurrence recovery are more verbose and tend to add bounds-check friction for no asymptotic benefit.
Compiled loops are adequate, yet slice bounds checks and runtime overhead are unnecessary for this small hot path.
Primitive arrays can implement the same recurrence, but JIT/runtime startup and managed array checks do not help a one-shot small-dimensional computation.
The recurrence algorithm is small enough to run, but top-percentile LeetCode performance favors native modular arithmetic and avoids VM/interpreter overhead.

variants.approachJustification: A valid array is exactly an alternating sequence of comparison signs. Rank symmetry reduces the two-direction DP to one vector u[y], where the next layer is a reversed suffix-sum transform. That transform is linear with dimension at most m, so the answer sequence satisfies a linear recurrence of order at most m. Generate enough initial terms with O(m) DP layers, recover the minimal recurrence with Berlekamp-Massey, and evaluate the requested length with Kitamasa.
variants.timeComplexity: O(m^2 log n + m^2) after O(m^2) term generation; d <= m is the recovered recurrence order

reasoningSummary: Adjacent inequality makes each step either up or down; forbidding strictly monotone triples forces these signs to alternate. The rank DP from Number of ZigZag Arrays I still applies, but n is now too large for layer-by-layer iteration. Since the mirrored DP applies the same fixed linear transform at every step, the total count sequence obeys a short linear recurrence. Berlekamp-Massey recovers it from generated prefix terms, and Kitamasa evaluates the n-th term modulo 1e9+7.
timeComplexity: O(m^2 log n), where m = r - l + 1 and m <= 75
